blocks.text: ON SURFACES AND THEIR CONTOURS
Roberto Pignoni
We prove two theorems concerning the global behaviour of a smooth compact
surface \(S\), without boundary, embedded in a real projective space or mapped to a
plane. Our starting point is an analysis of the orientability properties of the normal
bundle of a singular projective curve. Then we see how an excellent projection from \(S\)
to the Euclidean plane gives rise to integral relations linking the singularities of the
apparent contour. Finally, given an embedding of \(S\) in \(\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\), we look at the
discriminant \(\Delta^{*}\) of a net of hyperplanes that intersects \(S\) in a generic way, obtaining a
characterization of \(\Delta^{*}\) in terms of mod. 2 cohomology invariants.
0. Introduction
In this paper we undertake a study of surfaces, developed from a global point of
view by considering apparent contours and their duals. Our aim is to illustrate the
interrelation between some features of these curves and the topology of the surface to
which they belong. We are led, in this way, into a region that has been depicted as "a
mixture of integral and mod. 2 phenomena" \([\mathrm{K}]\).
The three sections of this article are meant to unfold different aspects of the subject.
Section 1 concerns general properties of curves with cusps and double points in a
real projective plane. Some very appropriate remarks of the referee (unknown to us, but
whose qualified criticism has been of great help in smoothing the style and improving the
structure of this paper) have stimulated us to redesign this part. We have introduced a new
and simple presentation of a few basic topological facts concerning singular projective
curves. It may be of interest that the present approach leads to a straightforward proof of a
slight refinement (Proposition 3) of a celebrated theorem of Möbius [Mö]. Our argument
is essentially topological (a discussion of related aspects can be found in [Po]).
In Section 2 we apply to apparent contours our results on singular projective curves,
making use of some Morse theoretical instruments introduced by Banchoff and Kuiper
PIGNONI
[B-K]. Then we sacrifice a line in the target of our mappings and switch from projective
to Euclidean plane. New possibilities are gained, due to the orientation properties that
become available. We focus our attention on generic profiles of surfaces: it is well known
that an irreducible contour in \(R^{2}\) can be generated by a surface (which may, or may not,
be orientable) of arbitrarily high genus (Fig.1). For this reason, at first sight there seems
to be little hope of finding a significant connection between the number of irreducible
components displayed by the contour and the genus of the surface.
Fig. 1
Our purpose is to show that such a connection exists, provided we count the
components in the right way. Theorem 1 establishes a relation between the genus \(g\) of the
surface and the number of cusps, double points and irreducible components (suitably
counted) presented by its generic apparent contour. It outlines also a new procedure,
alternative to the one described in Proposition 7, enabling us to recover the Euler-Poincare
characteristic of a surface by looking at its oriented apparent contour in the Euclidean
plane (our indebtment with the classical theory of the rotation number of a plane curve,
due to Whitney [W1], is self-evident). For a first application of this result, see [B-D];
more consequences shall be discussed in a forthcoming paper [Pi2].
PIGNONI
In Section 3 we consider surfaces embedded in \(n\)-dimensional real projective space.
We intersect them with a generic linear net of hyperplanes, giving a cohomological
description of the discriminant defined in this operation (Theorem 2). The information
obtained in this way can be used in order to characterize, by means of duality arguments,
the second Stiefel-Whitney number of the surface in terms of the cusps of a generic
mapping with values in \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\) (Corollary 2). The reader may suppose there is some kind of
affinity between our approach to the topology of a surface embedded in RP \({ }^{n}\), investigated
by means of intersection with a net of hyperplanes, and some well known constructions
of complex algebraic geometry. The theory of Lefschetz pencils, for instance, as
developed by Andreotti and Frankel [A-F]. However, the technical foundations for the
study undertaken in Section 3 rest rather on Looijenga's theory of generic families of
functions [Lo] and on John Mather's results on generic projections [Ma].
The first formulation of some concepts introduced in this paper had been outlined in
joint work with Alessandro Orlandi [O-P]. Partial results were presented, without proof,
in [Pi1]. On that occasion we sketched a first survey of some literature connected with
these subjects.
1. Singular Curves in a Projective Plane
Throughout this paper we will be concerned with a class of curves which may
present singularities. They are described in the following
Definition 1 A compact connected subset \(\gamma\) in \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\) is a curve with cusps and

double points if \(\forall \mathrm{x} \in \gamma \exists \mathrm{U}\) open in \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\), with \(\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{U}\), and a diffeomorphism \(\psi: \mathrm{U} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{2}\),
such that \(\psi(\mathrm{U} \cap \gamma)\) is :
1) either the set \(\left\{(x, y) \in R^{2} \mid y=0\right\}\);
2) or the set \(\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \in \mathrm{R}^{2} \mid \mathrm{xy}=0\right\}\);
3) or finally the set \(\left\{(x, y) \in R^{2} \mid x^{2}-y^{3}=0\right\}\).
We say that such a curve is irreducible if it can be parametrized by one smooth \(\left(C^{\infty}\right)\)
map \(\varphi: \mathrm{S}^{1}->\mathrm{RP}^{2}\), which is singular only when the image is a cusp point.
In a parametrization of \(\gamma\) we will assume that \(S^{1}\) is oriented and it is assigned a base
point \(a\), such that \(\varphi(a)\) is not an inflection point. If \(p_{1}\) precedes \(p_{2}\) in \(S^{1}\) (that is, moving
from \(a\) in the sense prescribed by the orientation, we meet \(p_{1}\) first) we will write \(p_{1}<p_{2}\).
If we remove from \(\gamma\) every cusp and double point, we are left with a finite number
of connected arcs, which are submanifolds of \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\). Let's fix an orientation for the normal
bundle of each of these arcs and see what happens near the singularities (Fig.2).
If \(x\) is a cusp point, orientations have been assigned to the two different arcs which
contain \(x\) in their closure. We say they are coherent if, taken a small neighbourhood \(U\) of
PIGNONI
\(x\) such that \(\{U \backslash \gamma\}\) has two connected components, the positive direction always points
towards the same connected component.
If \(y \in \gamma\) is a double point, arising from a self-intersection of an irreducible
component \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\), take a smooth parametrization \(\varphi: S^{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{2}\) of \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\). There are two points \(p_{1}\),
\(p_{2} \in S^{1}, p_{1} \neq p_{2}\), such that \(y=\varphi\left(p_{1}\right)=\varphi\left(p_{2}\right)\). We can choose two disjoint connected
open subsets \(I_{1}, I_{2}\) in \(S^{1}\), with \(p_{\mathrm{i}} \in I_{\mathrm{i}}\), such that \(\varphi\left(I_{1}\right), \varphi\left(I_{2}\right)\) are submanifolds of \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\)
only intersecting at \(y\). We say an orientation of the normal bundles of the four arcs having
\(y\) in their closure is coherent (at \(y\) ) if it can be induced, in a neighbourhood of \(y\), by global
orientations of the normal bundles of \(\varphi\left(I_{1}\right)\) and \(\varphi\left(I_{2}\right)\). An analogous definition can be
given for a double point where two different irreducible components of \(\gamma\) intersect.
Fig. 2
Definition 2 A curve with oriented normal bundle \(\{\gamma, \nu\}\) will mean a curve \(\gamma\)
together with an orientation of the normal bundle of each component of the complement of
double points and cusps in \(\gamma\), such that the orientation is coherent, in the above sense,
across each cusp and double point .
Proposition 1 Let \(\gamma\) be a curve with cusps and double points in \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\), and let \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\)
be its irreducible components. The normal bundle \(v\) can be globally oriented if and only if
each \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\) is homotopic to zero.
PIGNONI
Proof Let \(r\) be an oriented projective line intersecting an irreducible component \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\)
in a generic way and let \(\left\{p_{1}, \ldots, p_{\mathrm{m}}\right\}\) be the elements of \(\left\{r \cap \gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\right\}\). If we take a tubular
neighbourhood \(N\) of \(r\) the orientation of \(r\) induces an orientation of \(\partial N\), as shown by
Fig.3-left.
Fig. 3
\(N\) can be chosen in such a way that, in the affine plane \(\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{2} \backslash r\right\}, \partial N\) is a simple
oriented closed curve containing all the singularities of \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\) in its interior and intersecting \(\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}\)
transversally in \(2 m\) points. Let \(\left\{\sigma_{1}, \ldots, \sigma_{m}\right\}\) be the irreducible arcs of \(\left\{\gamma_{i} \backslash N\right\}\). We can
orient \(\eta_{\sigma_{\mathrm{j}}}\) so that the orientation of its first fiber agrees with that of \(\partial N\) (Fig.3-right). The
resulting orientation at the last fiber of \(\eta_{\sigma_{\mathrm{j}}}\) will then disagree with that of \(\partial N\).
From this we see that \(v_{l_{i}}\) has a global coherent orientation \(\Leftrightarrow m\) is even. A
straightforward mod-2 homology argument shows that:
\(m\) is even \(\Leftrightarrow \gamma_{i}\) is homologous to zero \(\Leftrightarrow \gamma_{i}\) is homotopic to zero.
Let's restrict ourselves to curves (with cusps and double points) composed of a
finite number of convex arcs. We will call them "convex-generic". For this class of
curves, we shall explicitate the link between the orientability of \(v\) and the number of
inflections of \(\gamma . \mathbb{N}\) will denote the set of integers, endowed with the discrete topology.
Definition 3 Let \(\gamma\) be irreducible and convex-generic. We say i: \(\mathrm{S}^{1} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) is a
jump function for \(\gamma\) if there is a parametrization \(\varphi: S^{1} \rightarrow R^{2}\) of \(\gamma(\) associated to i) s.t.:
1) \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{i}) \subset\{1,0,-1\}\) and \(\mathrm{i}(\mathrm{t})=0 \Leftrightarrow \varphi(\mathrm{t})\) is an inflection point;
2) for \(\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}^{\prime}\) not corresponding to inflection points and such that \(\mathrm{t}<\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\), let's denote by

\(\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)\) the number of inflections \(\varphi(\mathrm{x})\) with \(\mathrm{t}<\mathrm{x}<\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\). Then: \(\mathrm{i}\left(\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)=(-1)^{\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)} \mathrm{i}(\mathrm{t})\).
Suppose \(\gamma\) admits a jump function. The correspondence: parametrization of \(\gamma<->\)
jump function on \(\gamma\) is many-to-one. It is clear that a jump function \(i\) is constant on each
PIGNONI
convex arc of \(\gamma\), and its value "jumps" by two every time we pass an inflection point.
From now on, we shall denote by \(\tau(p)\) the tangent line to \(\gamma\) at \(p\), defined also at cusp
points, and by \(f\) the number of inflections of the curve. We assume \(\gamma\) to be irreducible.
Lemma 1 A jump function i for \(\gamma\) exists \(\Leftrightarrow \mathrm{f}\) is even .
Proof The proof is immediate. Parametrize \(\gamma\) and fix the value of \(i\) on the base
point. There is just one way to extend this value to the whole curve: when we get back to
the base point, the function will be well defined if and only if \(f\) is even.
Lemma 2 The normal bundle \(v\) is orientable ⇔ \(\gamma\) admits a jump function .
Proof a) ⇒ Fix an orientation \(\omega\) on \(v\) and a parametrization \(\varphi\) of \(\gamma\). We can
define \(i(t)\) as follows. Let \(q=\varphi(t) \in \gamma\). We take a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(q\), which in a
suitable affine chart is a disk centered at \(q\), and such that \(\{U \backslash q\}\) does not contain any
double point, cusp or inflection point. Let's call \(D_{1}, \ldots, D_{k}\) the connected domains of
\(\left(\operatorname{RP}^{2} \backslash \gamma\right)\).
If \(q\) is not an inflection point, nor a singular point, we choose \(U\) so that \(\{U \backslash \gamma\}\) has
two connected components and only one of them is a convex set, which lies in one of the
\(D_{\mathrm{i}}\) 's. We call \(D_{\text {conv. }}(q)\) this \(D_{\mathrm{i}}\). We set \(i(t)=1\) if and only if the positive orientation of \(v\)
at \(q\) points towards \(D_{\text {conv. }}(q)\). At a cusp point \(q=\varphi(t)\) we set \(i(t)=1\) if at any sufficiently
close point \(q^{\prime}\) of \(\gamma\) the oriented normal bundle \(\nu\) heads towards \(D_{\text {conv. }}\left(q^{\prime}\right) ;-1\) otherwise.
At a double point we delete the branch of the curve which is transversal to the tangent
\(\varphi^{\prime}(t)\), and assign the value +1 or -1 in the same way.
b) \(<=i: S^{1} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) is given, and it is associated to a parametrization \(\varphi: S^{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{2}\) of
\(\gamma\). We orient the fibers of \(v\) at points \(q=\varphi(t)\) that are not inflections nor singular points,
by requiring \(v\) to point into \(D_{\text {conv. }}(q)\) if and only if \(i(t)=1\). This orientation extends in a
coherent way to all singularities and inflections of \(\gamma\).
Definition 4 We say that a jump function i is associated with a given orientation \(\omega\)
of \(v\) if they are related in the way described in the proof of Lemma 2, points \(\mathbf{a}\) ) and \(\mathbf{b )}\).
From what precedes, one immediately obtains (compare with [H-K, p.141]):
Proposition 2 Let \(\gamma\) be a curve with cusps and double points, irreducible and
convex-generic. Then: \(f\) is even \(\Leftrightarrow \gamma\) is homotopic to zero.
Proposition 2 and the proof of Proposition 1 can be a convenient starting point in
order to obtain a finer version of a theorem of Möbius [Mö, S, H-K]. The argument is
elementary in character, and has a topological basis.
PIGNONI
Proposition 3 Let \(\gamma\) be a simple closed curve in \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\) which is not contractible to
a point. Then \(\gamma\) must have at least three inflection points. Furthermore, if r is a line
intersecting \(\gamma\) in a generic way and disconnecting \(\gamma\), these inflections belong to more than
one connected component of \(\{\gamma \backslash \mathrm{r}\}\).
Proof Call \(m\) the number of intersections between \(r\) and \(\gamma\). Then \(m\) is necessarily
odd (see the proof of Proposition 1). We begin with the case \(m=1\). Take a "small"
tubular neighbourhood \(N\) of \(r\), whose border \(\partial N\) is a simple closed curve embedded in the
affine plane \(\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{2} \backslash r\right\}\) and intersecting \(\gamma\) transversally in two points \(q\) and \(q^{\prime}\). We may
assume \(\partial N\) to contain all inflections of \(\gamma\) in its interior (as in the proof of Proposition 1). If
\(p\) is the unique intersection between \(r\) and \(\gamma\), the respective position of \(\gamma\) and \(\tau(p)\) in a
neighbourhood of \(q\) and \(q^{\prime}\) is shown in Fig.4-left. Endow \(\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{2} \backslash r\right\}\) with an Euclidean
structure. If \(h\) is the height function in the direction normal to \(\tau(p)\), it must have a
maximum and a minimum on \(\gamma\) inside \(\partial N\). The sign of the curvature function changes at
least 3 times as we go from \(q\) to \(q^{\prime}\), hence \(\gamma\) must have at least three inflection points.
Fig. 4
Let's consider what happens when \(m>1\) and \(\{\gamma \backslash r\}\) has \(m\) different connected
components \(\left(\sigma_{1}, \ldots, \sigma_{m}\right)\). We only need to prove the second statement of Proposition 3,
because if two components contain inflections, these must be at least three. So, we are
going to show that it is impossible that all the \(\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}\) but one be convex. We set \(m=2 k+1\)
and carry on the proof by induction on \(k\).
Let's see the case \(k=1\). Let's suppose there is just one component with inflections
(call it \(\sigma_{1}\) ). On \(r, \sigma_{1}\) must have two distinct endpoints \(p\) and \(p^{\prime}\) for otherwise \(\gamma\) would be
disconnected. After orienting the normal bundle \(v\) of \(\sigma_{1}\), we associate a jump mapping
\(i:(0,1)->\mathbb{N}\) to this (open) curve with oriented normal bundle (the definition being
PIGNONI
patterned on that given for irreducible closed curves). Since \(\sigma_{1}\) has an odd number of
flexes and \(\gamma\) is simple, in a neighbourhood of \(p\) and \(p^{\prime}\) the portrait of the curve must be as
in Fig. 4 -right (the straight lines representing tangents relative to \(p\) and \(p\) ). But this makes
it impossible to draw more than one convex arc joining the third intersection point \(a\) with
the other two, contradicting the hypothesis that \(\gamma\) be a simple closed curve.
Now we are ready to prove our claim for \(k>1\). Again, let's suppose \(\sigma_{1}\) is the only
component of \((\gamma \backslash r)\) to display inflections, and let \(p\) and \(p^{\prime}\) be its distinct endpoints. Call
\(\sigma_{2}\) the convex component starting at \(p\). Between \(\sigma_{2}\) and \(r\) lies a convex domain \(C\) (see
Fig.5-left: again, the straight lines represent \(\tau(p)\) and \(\tau\left(p^{\prime}\right)\) ). In the closure of \(C\) lies at
least one connected domain of the same kind (the boundary being made of a convex arc \(\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}\)
and a segment of \(r\) ), but containing no convex components of \(\{\gamma \backslash r\}\) in its interior. This
\(\operatorname{arc} \sigma_{\mathrm{i}}\) can be deformed by means of a regular homotopy into a new convex arc \(\sigma^{\prime}\) not
meeting \(r\) (Fig.5-right). In this way we reduce ourselves to the case of \(m-2\) intersection
points and the induction mechanism is released.
Fig. 5
We will introduce now a different characterization for the orientability of the normal
bundle \(\nu\) of a singular curve \(\gamma\). We don't assume \(\gamma\) to be convex-generic, nor irreducible.
Definition 5 Let \(\gamma\) be a singular projective curve. A function \(\mathrm{f}: \mathrm{RP}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\) (as
before, \(\mathbb{N}\) denotes the set of integers, endowed with the discrete topology) will be called a
primitive function for \(\gamma\) if:
1) f is constant on the connected components \(\left\{\mathrm{D}_{1}, \ldots, \mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}\right\}\) of \(\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{2} \backslash \gamma\right\}\) and the
value of \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{p})\) jumps by two each time p crosses the curve transversely, at one of its regular
points, passing from \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{i}}\) to \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{j}}\);
PIGNONI
2) if we take a small convex neighbourhood U of a double point q of \(\gamma\), such that
\{Uy\} has 4 connected components, then \(f\) assumes three distinct values on these
components;
3) if \(\mathrm{p} \in \gamma, \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{p})\) is the average value of f for the (2 or 4) neighbouring regions \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{j}}\).
Proposition \(4 \gamma\) has a primitive function \(\Leftrightarrow\) its normal bundle \(v\) is orientable.
Proof a) ⇒ Let \(\varphi\) be a primitive function for \(\gamma\). We can fix an orientation of the
normal bundle \(v\) as follows. Let us take a point \(p \in \gamma\) that is not a double point, nor a

cusp. Then \(\exists j_{1}, j_{2}\) such that \(p\) belongs to \(\partial D_{\mathrm{j}_{1}} \cap \partial D_{\mathrm{j}_{2}}\) : if we take \(q_{1} \in D_{\mathrm{j}_{1}}\) and \(q_{2} \in D_{\mathrm{j}_{2}}\),
\(\varphi\left(q_{1}\right)\) differs from \(\varphi\left(q_{2}\right)\) by two. Let's suppose \(\varphi\left(q_{1}\right)=\varphi\left(q_{2}\right)+2\) : we assign to the fiber
of \(v\) in \(p\) the orientation pointing towards \(D_{\mathrm{j}_{1}}\). In order to check that such a choice of
orientation for each fiber of \(v\) defines a global orientation \(\omega\) of the bundle, not depending
on the initial choice of \(p\), it is sufficient to observe that points 1 ) and 2 ) of Definition 5
ensure that the orientations of the 4 arcs abutting on a double point fit together in a
coherent way.
b) \(<=\) Choose an orientation \(\omega\) for \(v\). Fix a point \(p\) not belonging to \(\gamma\). If \(q\) is
another point of \(\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{2}(y)\right.\), consider the two segments \(A_{1}\) and \(A_{2}\) joining \(p\) and \(q\). Orient
each of them in the sense that goes from \(p\) to \(q\) and assign an index to each point \(a\) at
which \(A_{\mathrm{j}}\) and \(\gamma\) intersect transversally: the index will be +2 if, at \(a\), the orientations of \(A_{\mathrm{j}}\)
and of \(v\) point to the same side of \(\gamma,-2\) otherwise. Now, the sum of all indexes relative to
\(A_{1}\) is equal to the sum of those relative to \(A_{2}\). In order to see this, pick an irreducible
component \(\gamma_{i}\) of \(\gamma\). Let \(m_{1}\) be the sum of all indexes relative to \(A_{1} \cap \gamma_{i}, m_{2}\) the sum of
those relative to \(A_{2} \cap \gamma_{1}\), and let's see that \(m_{1}=m_{2}\). Extend the orientation of \(A_{1}\) to the
whole line \(r\) passing through \(p\) and \(q\). Make a full loop along \(r\), starting and ending at \(p\)
and assigning to each transversal intersection with \(\gamma_{i}\) an index in the same way as before.
The sum of all indexes will be \(m_{1}-m_{2}\). By applying the same argument exploited in the
proof of Proposition 1, we see that this sum is zero, which entrains \(m_{1}=m_{2}\).
This enables us to define a primitive function \(\varphi\). We start with \(p\), setting \(\varphi(p)=0\)
(notice that the choice of this value is totally arbitrary). If \(q\) is a point of \(\left(\operatorname{RP}^{2} \gamma\right\}\), we take
a segment \(A\) joining \(p\) and \(q\), we orient it in the sense that goes from \(p\) to \(q\) and define
\(\varphi(q)\) to be the sum of all indexes relative to the transversal intersections between \(A\) and \(\gamma\),
as before. We obtain a well defined function \(\varphi:\left(\mathrm{RP}^{2} \mid y\right\} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\), which verifies points 1\()\)
and 2) of Definition 5 and can be extended to the points of \(\gamma\) as prescribed by point 3).
Definition 6 Let \(\varphi\) be a primitive function for \(\gamma, \omega\) an orientation of \(v\). If they are
related as in the proof of Proposition 4 (that is, when we cross \(\gamma\) following the positive
orientation of \(v\), the value of \(\varphi\) increases by two) we say they are associated.
A primitive function determines a well defined orientation of \(v\), but the converse is
not true. There are infinitely many primitive functions associated with the same orientation
\(\omega\) of \(v\) and any two of them differ by a constant. This is an easy consequence of the
construction used in the proof of Proposition 4 (part b)). We have shown:
Corollary 1 If v is orientable, there are two equivalence classes of primitive
functions for \(\gamma\). Two equivalent primitives differ by a constant, so that each primitive
function is determined by its equivalence class and by its value at one point of \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\).
Two primitives are equivalent ⇔ they are associated to the same orientation of \(v\)
⇔ they differ by a constant. If \(\varphi\) is a primitive for \(\gamma\) and \(a\) is an integer, \(\varphi+a\) is a
primitive function of the same class; \(-\varphi\) is a primitive function belonging to the other
equivalence class.
We have just remarked that there are infinitely many primitives associated with a
curve with oriented normal bundle \(\{\gamma, v\}\). However, if \(\gamma\) is convex-generic it is possible
to assign to \(\{\gamma, v\}\) a special primitive function \(\chi_{\gamma}\) which is defined in a canonical way.
Let's see how. At first, suppose \(\gamma\) irreducible and take a jump function \(i: S^{1} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}\),
associated with \(\nu s\) orientation (Definition 4) and with a parametrization \(\varphi: S^{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{2}\) of
\(\gamma\). If \(p \in \mathrm{RP}^{2}\), let \(C(p)\) denote the pencil of lines of center \(p\).
Definition 7 We set \(S(p)=\left\{t_{1}, \ldots, t_{\lambda} \in S^{1}\right.\) such that \(\left.\tau\left(\varphi\left(t_{i}\right)\right) \in \mathcal{L}(p)\right\}\). Then

\[
\chi_{\gamma}(p)=\sum_{t \in S(p)} i(t)
\]
The meaning of this new function is illustrated by Fig.6. The value of \(\chi_{\gamma}(p)\) is
equal to the sum of as many terms +1 as there are points \(q \in \gamma\) at which the tangent line
\(\tau(q)\) spans \(p\), with the positive orientation of \(v\) in \(q\) pointing towards \(D_{\text {conv. }}(q)\) (remember
the proof of Lemma 2); and of as many terms -1 as there are points \(q\) with tangent \(\tau(q)\)
spanning \(p\), but at which it is the negative orientation of \(v\) that heads towards \(D_{\text {conv. }}(q)\).
The value of an inflection point \(q\), with \(\tau(q)\) spanning \(p\), is 0 . A cusp point \(q\), whose
tangent spans \(p\), counts +1 when in any sufficiently close point \(q^{\prime}\) of \(\gamma\) the oriented normal
bundle \(v\) heads towards \(D_{\text {conv. }}\left(q^{\prime}\right) ;-1\) otherwise. Finally, in a double point we delete the
branch of the curve which is transversal to the tangent, and assign the value +1 or -1 in
the usual way.
If \(\gamma\) has \(h\) irreducible components \(\left\{\gamma_{1}, \ldots, \gamma_{\mathrm{h}}\right\}\) whose normal bundles have all been
oriented, we can extend the above definition by setting: \(\chi_{\gamma}=\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{h}} \chi_{\gamma_{\mathrm{i}}}\)
Proposition \(5 \quad \chi_{\gamma}(\mathrm{p})\) is a primitive function for \(\gamma\).
PIGNONI
Fig. 6
Proof We are dealing with an integral valued function. Let's see that its value
does not change as \(p\) varies within the same connected component \(D_{\mathrm{j}}\) of \(\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{2} \backslash \gamma\right\}\). We
need to show that \(\forall a, b \in D_{\mathrm{j}}\) one has \(\chi_{\gamma}(a)=\chi_{\gamma}(b)\). We will prove this statement
assuming that the two points abut a segment \(a b \subset D_{\mathrm{j}}\) not lying on any tangent to \(\gamma\) and
such that either: 1) \(a b\) does not intersect any tangent to an inflection point, or: 2) there is
just one such intersection (at an interior point of \(a b\) ). In fact, every couple of points in \(D_{\mathrm{j}}\)
can be connected by a polygonal path whose segments are of this kind, the only exception
occurring when one of the two points lies on the tangent to an inflection. And the
arguments we will give still apply (with minor adjustments) to this case.
So let's see how things work in case 1). Pick a line \(l \in \boldsymbol{L}(a)\), such that \(l=\tau(p)\) for
some \(p \in \gamma\). Parametrize \(\gamma\), take the two inflection points immediately preceding and
following \(p\) and call them \(f_{1}\) and \(f_{2}\), respectively. Let \(C\) be the arc of \(\gamma\) containing \(p\) and
going from \(f_{1}\) to \(f_{2}\). If we move along \(C\) starting from \(p\) and going in the proper direction,
the tangent \(\tau(q)\) will keep intersecting \(a b\) until it gets to \(b\) (which happens before \(q\) reaches
the inflection). \(i\) remains constant and the contribution of the \(\operatorname{arc} C\) to \(\chi_{\gamma}(x)\) is the same for
all \(x \in a b\). This argument can be repeated for all tangents \(l\) drawn from \(a\) to \(\gamma\), and can be
applied also when the component of \(\gamma\) containing \(p\) has no inflections. Case 1 ) is proved.
Now suppose there is a line \(\tau(p)\) tangent to an inflection point \(p\) and intersecting \(a b\)
at a point \(c\) (case 2 ). Again, let's take an open arc \(C\) of \(\gamma\) containing \(p\) and joining the two

2. Oriented Apparent Contours
Take a compact surface \(S\), connected and without boundary. If \(f: S \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{2}\) is a
generic mapping, its critical point set is the union of \(s\) circles \(C_{1}, \ldots, C_{\mathrm{s}}\) embedded in \(S\).
They constitute the fold curve of \(f\) and their images are the irreducible components of the
apparent contour \(\gamma\), which is a curve with cusps and double points [W2, Ma]. We are
going to see that \(f\) determines, in a "canonical" way, two distinct primitives for this curve.
They belong to different equivalence classes, hence their sum must be equal to a constant.
We will realize that this constant has a topological meaning, being equal to the Euler-
Poincaré characteristic \(\chi(S)\) of the surface.
Let's introduce the first "canonical" primitive function associated with the contour.

fold curve \(\sigma_{\mathrm{q}}\). By taking, near \(q\), a chart of type \(\left(x_{2}, t\right)\) we conclude that \(h\left(x_{2}, 0\right)=\psi\left(x_{2}\right)\)
must be such that \(\psi^{\prime \prime \prime}(0) \neq 0\).
Proposition 8 ensures that if the embedding \(\varphi\) and the center \(C\) are both chosen in a
generic way, \(\Delta^{*}\) and \(\Delta\) (or \(\gamma\) ) will be curves with cusps and double points, having a finite
number of ordinary inflections and of double tangents (none of them being tangent to an
inflection point), no triple tangents, etc. In particular, they are convex-generic.
Now suppose \(\Sigma^{*}\) is a linear net of hyperplanes in \(\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\), of center \(C\), which is regular
for \(\{S, \varphi\}\), and let \(\Delta^{*}=\left(\Sigma^{*}\right)_{1} \cup\left(\Sigma^{*}\right)_{0}\) be its discriminant. Let \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) be generators for

\(\mathrm{H}^{1}\left(\mathrm{RP}^{2} ; \mathrm{Z}_{2}\right)\) and \(\mathrm{H}^{2}\left(\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}} ; \mathrm{Z}_{2}\right)\), respectively. We indicate with \(\langle\sigma, \varphi(S)\rangle\) and \(\left\langle\mu, \Delta^{*}\right\rangle\)
their Kronecker product with the homology classes induced by \(\varphi(S)\) in \(\mathrm{H}_{2}\left(\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}} ; \mathrm{Z}_{2}\right)\) and
by \(\Delta^{*}\) in \(\mathrm{H}_{1}\left(\mathrm{RP}^{2} ; \mathrm{Z}_{2}\right)\). Furthermore, let \(w_{2}(S)\) be the second Stiefel-Whitney number of
\(S\), obtained by making the Kronecker product between the second Stiefel-Whitney class
\(w_{2}\) of the tangent bundle and the fundamental class of \(S\).
Theorem 2 For a generic embedding \(\varphi: \mathrm{S} \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\) and a regular net \(\Sigma^{*}\), we can
compute \(<\mu, \Delta^{*}>\) in the following way:

\[
\left\langle\mu, \Delta^{*}\right\rangle=\langle\sigma, \varphi(S)\rangle+w_{2}(S)
\]
Proof Let \(\Sigma\) be the family of all \((n-2)\)-hyperplanes containing \(C\) (that is, \(\Sigma\) is
the dual space of \(\Sigma^{*}\) ). Let's consider the projection of center \(C\); as usual, we represent \(\Sigma\)
and \(\Delta\) by means of a two-plane \(H\), in which \(\Delta\) is embodied as the apparent contour of \(S\),
labelled by \(\gamma\). Following our customary notations, we indicate with \(v\) the normal bundle of
\(\gamma\) and with \(m\) the primitive function that is canonically associated with the orientation of \(v\)
determined by the projection \(f: S->H\) (Definitions 8 and 9). Since \(\gamma\) is convex-generic,
\(\chi_{\gamma}\) (Definition 7) is defined on all of \(H\), and it represents the other "canonical" primitive
function induced by the oriented apparent contour. We have seen these two primitives
belong to different equivalence classes, which implies that their sum is constant (Corollary
1). In the generic setting we have ensured, this constant coincides with the Euler-Poincaré
characteristic \(\chi(S)\) of the surface (Proposition 7). Therefore the following relation holds:

\[
\chi_{\gamma}+m=\chi(S)
\]
Now we fix a point \(p\) belonging to \(\{H \backslash \gamma\}\). We draw all the tangents from \(p\) to \(\gamma\) and
denote their number by \(\alpha_{p}\). From the above relation, we immediately get:

\[
\alpha_{p}=\chi(S)+m(p) \quad(\bmod .2)
\]
Let \(r\) be the line, contained in \(\Sigma^{*}\), which is dual to the element of \(\Sigma\) represented by
\(p\). We call \(m_{r}\) the number of points at which this line intersects \(\Delta^{*}\). Since \(m_{r}=\alpha_{p}\), we
obtain a dual formula:
PIGNONI

\[
m_{r}=\chi(S)+m(p) \quad(\bmod .2)
\]
Let \(T \in \Sigma\) be the ( \(n-2\) )-hyperplane containing \(p\) and \(C . T\) and \(\varphi(S)\) lie in general
position and \(m(p)\) tells us how many times the embedded surface \(\varphi(S) \subset \mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\) intersects \(T\).
We observe that, by applying Poincaré duality:
1) \(m_{r}\) is even \(\Leftrightarrow<\mu, \Delta^{*}>=0\)
2) \(m(p)\) is even \(\Leftrightarrow<\sigma, \varphi(S)>=0\)
We recall that the second Stiefel-Whitney number \(w_{2}(S)\) of \(S\) is equal to the Euler-
Poincaré characteristic of the surface, reduced mod.2. This final remark concludes the
proof of Theorem 2.
Theorem 2 can be used in order to extend to mappings to the projective plane some
classical results on the number of cusps of a generic map. The theorems available in the
literature concern maps taking their values in a Euclidean plane [W2, T1, H], as well as in
any orientable surface \([\mathrm{LI}, \mathrm{E}]\) (a more general relation is quoted, without proof, in \([\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{I}]\) ).
So, let \(S\) be a compact connected surface without boundary: we are going to see how the
second Stiefel-Whitney class \(w_{2}\) of the tangent bundle of \(S\) is reflected by the global
structure of a generic (stable) smooth mapping \(f: S \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{2}\). More precisely, we will
prove that \(w_{2}\) is dual to the 0 -cycle defined by the cusps and by a generic fiber \(f^{-1}(p)\) of \(f\).
Corollary 2 Let \(\mathrm{d}^{*}(\mathrm{f})\) denote the mod. 2 degree of \(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{c}^{*}\) the number of cusps
\(\left(\right.\) mod.2), and \(\chi^{*}(S)\) the mod. 2 Euler-Poincaré characteristic of the surface. Then :
Proof We embed \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\) as a projective subspace of \(\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}, n \geq 5\). Let \(j: \mathrm{RP}^{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\)
be this embedding and set \(H=\operatorname{Im} j\). We fix a center of projection \(P \in \operatorname{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\) (an ( \(n-3\) )-
hyperplane \(P\) not intersecting \(H\) ) and consider the map \(\pi:\left\{\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}} \backslash P\right\} \rightarrow H\) introduced
before. Let \(N\) be a neighbourhood of \(f\) in the space of smooth mappings from \(S\) to \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\),
chosen in such a way that \(\forall g \in N, g\) is stable and smoothly equivalent (and homotopic)
to \(f\). The map \(j\) of can be approximated by a generic mapping \(f^{\prime}: S \rightarrow \mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}}\) belonging to the
class \(\mathcal{A}\) of embeddings introduced at the beginning of this section, and having the
property that \(\pi \circ f^{\prime} \in N\). This ensures that the number \(c\) of the cusps in the apparent
contours of \(f\) and \(\pi \circ f^{\prime}\) is the same. We can take a new center of projection \(C\) close to \(P\),
so that the corresponding map \(\pi^{\prime}\) of' approximates \(\pi \circ f^{\prime}\) arbitrarily well and its apparent
contour \(\gamma^{\prime}\) is convex-generic, has only ordinary inflections, etc. (Proposition 8). Since
\(\pi f^{\prime}\) is a stable mapping, the number of cusps in the apparent contour will keep the same.
Let \(\Delta^{*}\) be the discriminant of the net \(\Sigma^{*}\) defined by the new center \(C\) and
intersecting \(f^{\prime}(S)\). We have (Theorem 2): \(\left\langle\mu, \Delta^{*}\right\rangle=\left\langle\sigma, f^{\prime}(S)\right\rangle+w_{2}(S)\).
We observe that: \(<\mu, \Delta^{*}>=0 \Leftrightarrow\) the number of irreducible components of \(\Delta^{*}\)
not homologous to zero is even \(\Leftrightarrow\) the number of flexes of \(\Delta^{*}\) is even (Proposition 2).

The number of flexes of \(\Delta^{*}\) is equal to the number \(c\) of cusps of \(f\); in addition, for \(f^{\prime}\)
close to \(j\) of, the two cycles \(j\) of \((S)\) and \(f^{\prime}(S)\) lie in the same homology class. Now recall
that we denoted by \(c^{*}\) the number ( \(\bmod .2\) ) of cusp points of \(f\). Since \(w_{2}(S)=\chi^{*}(S), c^{*}\)
verifies the following relation: \(c^{*}=\langle\sigma, j \circ f(S)\rangle+\chi^{*}(S)\).
The homology class defined by \(j \circ f(S)\) in \(\mathrm{H}_{2}\left(\mathrm{RP}^{\mathrm{n}} ; \mathrm{Z}_{2}\right)\) is zero if and only if the
mod. 2 degree of \(f\) is zero, and this remark concludes the proof.
Remark 2 In order to realize the meaning of the relation we have just proved, we
can think of some examples of surfaces embedded in \(\mathrm{RP}^{3}\) and projected to an hyperplane
\(H\). Let \(g\) be the genus of \(S\) and denote by \([S]\) the homology class defined by the surface in
\(\mathrm{H}_{2}\left(\mathrm{RP}^{3} ; \mathrm{Z}_{2}\right)\). Let's put ourselves in an affine chart and look at the contour. For orientable
surfaces, the preceding result says that \(c\) must be even. In fact, we have already remarked
that a two-sheeted hyperboloid, standardly embedded and projected in the direction of its
axis, engenders an empty contour. A one-sheeted hyperboloid produces a circle. In both
cases \(c\) is "even", being equal to zero.
Turning to the non-orientable case we have: for \(g\) odd, \([S]=0 \Leftrightarrow c\) is odd; for \(g\)
even \([S]=0 \Leftrightarrow c\) is even. Fig. 1 gives some examples with \([S]=0\). To get an example
with \([S] \neq 0\), just remember that \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\), embedded as an hyperplane of \(\mathrm{RP}^{3}\), has an empty
contour. Hence \(g\) is odd, \(c\) even. Other examples for \(\mathrm{RP}^{2}\) are given in Fig. 12 (top): we
get the same contour that was seen in Fig.8, but in the present case \(g=1\) (odd), \(c=2\)
(even) and \([S] \neq 0\). To the bottom of Fig. 12 we see the contour of a surface of genus 6,
embedded in \(\mathrm{RP}{ }^{4}\). It is obtained by joining a two-dimensional hyperplane, by means of
three handles, to another projective plane. The last is embedded in an affine chart, just like
a generic 4-dimensional shadow of the Veronese surface [Ba, Fig.3], whose projection to
the plane gives rise to the same familiar contour as the "Roman surface" [A, F3] or Boy
surface's "flat view" [F-T1, Fig.3.2]. Here \(g\) is even, \(c\) odd and \(S\) is not homologous to
zero.
